sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Cellulitis is a bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, commonly caused by streptococci or Staphylococcus aureus, usually after a break in the skin.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness
- Lymphangitis or regional lymphadenopathy
- Possible discharge or exudate
- Fever, rigors, or sepsis in more severe disease

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Risk is increased by diabetes, lymphoedema, immunosuppression, vascular disease, skin disease, bites, ulcers, and poor self-care
- Facial, periorbital, hand, or joint-related infection needs urgent review
- Many conditions can mimic cellulitis, especially when itch predominates or pain is minimal

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Vital signs, including temperature, heart rate, BGL, blood pressure, and SpO2
- Secondary Survey
- Consider outlining the affected area to monitor spread
- Consider images, with consent, for review by on-call doctor
- Consider oxygen if septic and indicated
- Consider simple analgesia or antipyretic if needed for discomfort

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access
- Consider blood draw and point-of-care testing if indicated
- Consider fluid resuscitation if indicated
- 12 lead ECG if indicated
- Antibiotic choice should be guided by the on-call doctor and local protocol
- If septic, follow sepsis pathway

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Erysipelas is more superficial and often well demarcated; cellulitis is deeper and more diffuse
- Mimics include contact dermatitis, allergic reaction, venous stasis change, gout, and other inflammatory dermatoses
- Any clinical images must be consented to and handled per record policy
